<br/><br/>## Step 4: Load the Model with Transformers<br/><br/>`from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer` `import torch` `model_id = "moonshotai/Kimi-K2-Instruct"` `tokenizer = Simple Python Usage Example<br/>* Tips for a Smooth Experience<br/>* Why Choose LightNode for Your Deployment?<br/>* Final Thoughts<br/><br/># How to Run Kimi-K2-Instruct Locally: A Comprehensive Guide<br/><br/>Running **Kimi-K2-Instruct** locally can seem daunting at first — but with the right tools and steps, it’s surprisingly straightforward. Whether you’re a developer looking to experiment with advanced AI models or someone who wants full control over inference without relying on cloud APIs, this guide will walk you through the entire process step-by-step.<br/><br/>---<br/><br/>## What is Kimi-K2-Instruct?<br/><br/>Kimi-K2-Instruct is an advanced AI language model by Moonshot AI, designed for instruction-following tasks. It supports chat completion and is optimized for various inference engines like vLLM, SGLang, KTransformers, and TensorRT-LLM. The model is compatible with OpenAI and Anthropic style APIs, making it flexible for integration with existing tools.<br/><br/>---<br/><br/>## Why Run Kimi-K2-Instruct Locally?<br/><br/>* **Privacy & Control:** Keep data on your machine without sending info to third-party APIs.<br/>* **Customization:** Modify prompts, parameters, and pipelines as you like.<br/>* **Cost-Effective:** Avoid ongoing cloud inference fees.<br/>* **Speed:** Deploy on local powerful GPUs to reduce latency.<br/><br/>If you want to seriously push the boundaries of local AI inference, Kimi-K2-Instruct offers a powerful foundation.<br/><br/>---<br/><br/>## Step-by-Step: How to Run Kimi-K2-Instruct Locally<br/><br/>### 1\. Prepare Your Environment<br/><br/>Kimi-K2-Instruct benefits from GPU acceleration, so prepare a machine with a CUDA-enabled NVIDIA GPU and up-to-date drivers.<br/><br/>* **Install Docker Desktop** (for containerized deployment ease)<br/>* **Set up Python environment** with at least Python 3.8+<br/>* Install Python dependencies:<br/><br/>```<br/>pip install blobfile torch<br/>```<br/><br/>_Tip:_ You may also need to install specific inference engines like TensorRT-LLM or vLLM depending on your deployment choice.<br/><br/>---<br/><br/>### 2\. Download the Model Checkpoints<br/><br/>The model weights for Kimi-K2-Instruct are available in block-fp8 format on Hugging Face:<br/><br/>* Visit: `https://huggingface.co/moonshotai/Kimi-K2-Instruct`<br/>* Use the Hugging Face CLI to authenticate and download locally:<br/><br/>```<br/>huggingface-cli login<br/>huggingface-cli download moonshotai/Kimi-K2-Instruct --local-dir ./models/Kimi-K2-Instruct<br/>```<br/><br/>Make sure your `.env` or config files point to this directory, for example:<br/><br/>```<br/>MODEL_PATH=./models/Kimi-K2-Instruct<br/>DEVICE=cuda<br/>```<br/><br/>---<br/><br/>### 3\. Choose Your Inference Engine & Deployment Mode<br/><br/>Kimi-K2-Instruct supports multiple inference engines:<br/><br/>\| Engine            \| Notes                                          \| Recommended For                               \|<br/>\| ----------------- \| ---------------------------------------------- \| --------------------------------------------- \|<br/>\| **vLLM**          \| Efficient LLM serving; good for chat workloads \| Simpler multi-user applications               \|<br/>\| **SGLang**        \| Language model serving framework               \| Developers seeking lightweight deployment     \|<br/>\| **KTransformers** \| Lightweight, Rust-based; fast & low-resource   \| Edge devices or resource-limited environments \|<br/>\| **TensorRT-LLM**  \| Highly optimized GPU inference with multi-node \| High-performance, multi-GPU setups            \|<br/><br/>A popular setup for maximum speed is TensorRT-LLM, which supports multi-node distributed serving using `mpirun`.<br/><br/>---<br/><br/>### 4\. Example: Running with TensorRT-LLM in Docker<br/><br/>* First, build or get the TensorRT-LLM docker image with Kimi-K2-Instruct integrated.<br/>* Run the container with GPU passthrough, mounting your model directory:<br/><br/>```<br/>docker run -it --gpus all \<br/>  --name kimi-k2-instruct \<br/>  -v $(pwd)/models/Kimi-K2-Instruct:/models/Kimi-K2-Instruct \<br/>  -e MODEL_PATH=/models/Kimi-K2-Instruct \<br/>  -e DEVICE=cuda \<br/>  your-tensorrt-llm-image<br/>```<br/><br/>For multi-node inference (useful in large-scale inference):<br/><br/>* Ensure passwordless SSH between nodes.<br/>* Run:<br/><br/>```<br/>mpirun -np 2 -host host1,host2 \<br/>  docker exec -it kimi-k2-instruct some_inference_command<br/>```<br/><br/>_Note:_ Consult the TensorRT-LLM deployment guide for detailed commands.<br/><br/>---<br/><br/>### 5\. Simple Python Usage Example<br/><br/>If you want to interact with the model programmatically:<br/><br/>```<br/>from kimia_infer.api.kimia import KimiAudio  # or replace with appropriate Kimi-K2 class<br/>import torch<br/><br/>device = "cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu"<br/>model_path = "./models/Kimi-K2-Instruct"<br/><br/>model = KimiAudio(model_path=model_path)<br/>model.to(device)<br/><br/>messages = [    \{"role": "system", "content": "You are Kimi, an AI assistant created by Moonshot AI."\},<br/>    \{"role": "user", "content": [\{"type": "text", "text": "Please give a brief self-introduction."\}]\}<br/>]<br/><br/>response = model.chat_completion(messages, temperature=0.6, max_tokens=256)<br/>print(response)<br/>```<br/><br/>Adjust the import and class as per the latest Kimi-K2-Instruct API.<br/><br/>---<br/><br/>## Tips for a Smooth Experience<br/><br/>* **Set temperature to \~0.6** for best balance between creativity and relevance.<br/>* Always test your setup with small inputs before scaling.<br/>* Join Moonshot AI community or contact \[email protected\] for help.<br/>* Keep drivers, CUDA, and Docker up-to-date.<br/>* Monitor GPU utilization to maximize performance.<br/><br/>---<br/><br/>## Why Choose LightNode for Your Deployment?<br/><br/>Running Kimi-K2-Instruct demands reliable, high-performance servers — especially if you want to avoid bottlenecks in GPU resources or networking. That’s where LightNode comes in.<br/><br/>**LightNode’s GPU servers** are optimized for AI workloads — offering:<br/><br/>* Latest NVIDIA GPUs with plenty of VRAM<br/>* Fast network and disk IO for loading large model checkpoints<br/>* Flexible scaling as your application grows<br/><br/>I personally found their setup ideal for local inference tasks and seamless model deployment. You can get started with LightNode now to power your Kimi-K2-Instruct local runs!<br/><br/>---<br/><br/>## Final Thoughts<br/><br/>Running **Kimi-K2-Instruct locally** unlocks enormous potential for experimentation, privacy, and cost savings. Basics<br/><br/># 🌙Kimi K2: How to Run Locally<br/><br/>Guide on running Kimi K2 on your own local device!<br/><br/>Kimi K2 is the world’s most powerful open-source model, setting new SOTA performance in knowledge, reasoning, coding, and agentic tasks. The full 1T parameter model from Moonshot AI requires 1.09TB of disk space, while the quantized **Unsloth Dynamic 1.8-bit** version reduces this to just 245GB (-80% size)**:** **Kimi-K2-GGUF**<br/><br/>All uploads use Unsloth Dynamic 2.0 for SOTA 5-shot MMLU and KL Divergence performance, meaning you can run quantized LLMs with minimal accuracy loss.<br/><br/>Run in llama.cpp<br/><br/>## <br/><br/>⚙️ Recommended Settings<br/><br/>You need **250GB of disk space** at least to run the 1bit quant!<br/><br/>The only requirement is `**disk space + RAM + VRAM ≥ 250GB**`. That means you do not need to have that much RAM or VRAM (GPU) to run the model, but it will just be slower.<br/><br/>The 1.8-bit (UD-TQ1\_0) quant will fit in a 1x 24GB GPU (with all MoE layers offloaded to system RAM or a fast disk). Expect around 5 tokens/s with this setup if you have bonus 256GB RAM as well. The full Kimi K2 Q8 quant is 1.09TB in size and will need at least 8 x H200 GPUs.<br/><br/>For optimal performance you will need at least **250GB unified memory or 250GB combined RAM+VRAM** for 5+ tokens/s. If you have less than 250GB combined RAM+VRAM, then the speed of the model will definitely take a hit.<br/><br/>**If you do not have 250GB of RAM+VRAM, no worries!** llama.cpp inherently has **disk offloading**, so through mmaping, it'll still work, just be slower - for example before you might get 5 to 10 tokens / second, now it's under 1 token.<br/><br/>We suggest using our **UD-Q2\_K\_XL (381GB)** quant to balance size and accuracy!<br/><br/>For the best performance, have your VRAM + RAM combined = the size of the quant you're downloading. If not, it'll still work via disk offloading, just it'll be slower!<br/><br/>### <br/><br/>🌙 Official Recommended Settings:<br/><br/>According to Moonshot AI, these are the recommended settings for Kimi K2 inference:<br/><br/>* Set the **temperature 0.6** to reduce repetition and incoherence.<br/><br/>We recommend setting **min\_p to 0.01** to suppress the occurrence of unlikely tokens with low probabilities.<br/><br/>## <br/><br/>🔢 Chat template and prompt format<br/><br/>Kimi Chat does use a BOS (beginning of sentence token). The system, user and assistant roles are all enclosed with `<\|im_middle\|>` which is interesting, and each get their own respective token `<\|im_system\|>, <\|im_user\|>, <\|im_assistant\|>`.<br/><br/>Copy<br/><br/>```<br/><\|im_system\|>system<\|im_middle\|>You are a helpful assistant<\|im_end\|><\|im_user\|>user<\|im_middle\|>What is 1+1?<\|im_end\|><\|im_assistant\|>assistant<\|im_middle\|>2<\|im_end\|><br/>```<br/><br/>To separate the conversational boundaries (you must remove each new line), we get:<br/><br/>Copy<br/><br/>```<br/><\|im_system\|>system<\|im_middle\|>You are a helpful assistant<\|im_end\|><br/><\|im_user\|>user<\|im_middle\|>What is 1+1?<\|im_end\|><br/><\|im_assistant\|>assistant<\|im_middle\|>2<\|im_end\|><br/>```<br/><br/>## <br/><br/>💾 Model uploads<br/><br/>**ALL our uploads** \- including those that are not imatrix-based or dynamic, utilize our calibration dataset, which is specifically optimized for conversational, coding, and reasoning tasks.<br/><br/>MoE Bits<br/><br/>Type + Link<br/><br/>Disk Size<br/><br/>Details<br/><br/>1.66bit<br/><br/>UD-TQ1\_0<br/><br/>**245GB**<br/><br/>1.92/1.56bit<br/><br/>1.78bit<br/><br/>UD-IQ1\_S<br/><br/>**281GB**<br/><br/>2.06/1.56bit<br/><br/>1.93bit<br/><br/>UD-IQ1\_M<br/><br/>**304GB**<br/><br/>2.5/2.06/1.56<br/><br/>2.42bit<br/><br/>UD-IQ2\_XXS<br/><br/>**343GB**<br/><br/>2.5/2.06bit<br/><br/>2.71bit<br/><br/>UD-Q2\_K\_XL<br/><br/>**381GB**<br/><br/> 3.5/2.5bit<br/><br/>3.12bit<br/><br/>UD-IQ3\_XXS<br/><br/>**417GB**<br/><br/> 3.5/2.06bit<br/><br/>3.5bit<br/><br/>UD-Q3\_K\_XL<br/><br/>**452GB**<br/><br/> 4.5/3.5bit<br/><br/>4.5bit<br/><br/>UD-Q4\_K\_XL<br/><br/>**588GB**<br/><br/> 5.5/4.5bit<br/><br/>5.5bit<br/><br/>UD-Q5\_K\_XL<br/><br/>**732GB**<br/><br/>6.5/5.5bit<br/><br/>We've also uploaded versions in BF16 format.<br/><br/>## <br/><br/>🐢Run Kimi K2 Tutorials:<br/><br/>### <br/><br/>✨ Run in llama.cpp<br/><br/>1. Obtain the latest `llama.cpp` on GitHub here and switch to the PR 14654 or you can **use our** **current fork** **which should also work**. We expect mainline llama.cpp to integrate full support in the next few days! You can follow the build instructions below as well. Change `-DGGML_CUDA=ON` to `-DGGML_CUDA=OFF` if you don't have a GPU or just want CPU inference.<br/><br/>Copy<br/><br/>```<br/>apt-get update<br/>apt-get install pciutils build-essential cmake curl libcurl4-openssl-dev -y<br/>git clone https://github.com/unslothai/llama.cpp<br/>cmake llama.cpp -B llama.cpp/build \<br/>    -DBUILD_SHARED_LIBS=OFF -DGGML_CUDA=ON -DLLAMA_CURL=ON<br/>cmake --build llama.cpp/build --config Release -j --clean-first --target llama-quantize llama-cli llama-gguf-split llama-mtmd-cli<br/>cp llama.cpp/build/bin/llama-* llama.cpp<br/>```<br/><br/>1. If you want to use `llama.cpp` directly to load models, you can do the below: (:UD-IQ1\_S) is the quantization type. You can also download via Hugging Face (point 3). This is similar to `ollama run` . Use `export LLAMA_CACHE="folder"` to force `llama.cpp` to save to a specific location.<br/><br/>Please try out `-ot ".ffn_.*_exps.=CPU"` to offload all MoE layers to the CPU! This effectively allows you to fit all non MoE layers on 1 GPU, improving generation speeds. You can customize the regex expression to fit more layers if you have more GPU capacity.<br/><br/>If you have a bit more GPU memory, try `-ot ".ffn_(up\|down)_exps.=CPU"` This offloads up and down projection MoE layers.<br/><br/>Try `-ot ".ffn_(up)_exps.=CPU"` if you have even more GPU memory. This offloads only up projection MoE layers.<br/><br/>And finally offload all layers via `-ot ".ffn_.*_exps.=CPU"` This uses the least VRAM.<br/><br/>You can also customize the regex, for example `-ot "\.(6\|7\|8\|9\|[0-9][0-9]\|[0-9][0-9][0-9])\.ffn_(gate\|up\|down)_exps.=CPU"` means to offload gate, up and down MoE layers but only from the 6th layer onwards.<br/><br/>Copy<br/><br/>```<br/>export LLAMA_CACHE="unsloth/Kimi-K2-Instruct-GGUF"<br/>./llama.cpp/llama-cli \<br/>    -hf unsloth/Kimi-K2-Instruct-GGUF:TQ1_0 \<br/>    --cache-type-k q4_0 \<br/>    --threads -1 \<br/>    --n-gpu-layers 99 \<br/>    --temp 0.6 \<br/>    --min_p 0.01 \<br/>    --ctx-size 16384 \<br/>    --seed 3407 \<br/>    -ot ".ffn_.*_exps.=CPU"<br/>```<br/><br/>1. Download the model via (after installing `pip install huggingface_hub hf_transfer` ). You can choose `UD-TQ1_0`(dynamic 1.8bit quant) or other quantized versions like `Q2_K_XL` . We **recommend using our 2bit dynamic quant** `**UD-Q2_K_XL**` **to balance size and accuracy**. More versions at: huggingface.co/unsloth/Kimi-K2-Instruct-GGUF<br/><br/>Copy<br/><br/>```<br/># !pip install huggingface_hub hf_transfer<br/>import os<br/>os.environ["HF_HUB_ENABLE_HF_TRANSFER"] = "0" # Can sometimes rate limit, so set to 0 to disable<br/>from huggingface_hub import snapshot_download<br/>snapshot_download(<br/>    repo_id = "unsloth/Kimi-K2-Instruct-GGUF",<br/>    local_dir = "unsloth/Kimi-K2-Instruct-GGUF",<br/>    allow_patterns = ["*UD-TQ1_0*"], # Dynamic 1bit (281GB) Use "*UD-Q2_K_XL*" for Dynamic 2bit (381GB)<br/>)<br/>```<br/><br/>If you find that downloads get stuck at 90 to 95% or so, please see <https://docs.unsloth.ai/basics/troubleshooting-and-faqs#downloading-gets-stuck-at-90-to-95><br/><br/>1. Run any prompt.<br/>2. Edit `--threads -1` for the number of CPU threads (be default it's set to the maximum CPU threads), `--ctx-size 16384` for context length, `--n-gpu-layers 99` for GPU offloading on how many layers. Set it to 99 combined with MoE CPU offloading to get the best performance. Try adjusting it if your GPU goes out of memory. Also remove it if you have CPU only inference.<br/><br/>Copy<br/><br/>```<br/>./llama.cpp/llama-cli \<br/>    --model unsloth/Kimi-K2-Instruct-GGUF/UD-TQ1_0/Kimi-K2-Instruct-UD-TQ1_0-00001-of-00005.gguf \<br/>    --cache-type-k q4_0 \<br/>    --threads -1 \<br/>    --n-gpu-layers 99 \<br/>    --temp 0.6 \<br/>    --min_p 0.01 \<br/>    --ctx-size 16384 \<br/>    --seed 3407 \<br/>    -ot ".ffn_.*_exps.=CPU" \<br/>    -no-cnv \<br/>    --prompt "<\|im_system\|>system<\|im_middle\|>You are a helpful assistant<\|im_end\|><\|im_user\|>user<\|im_middle\|>Create a Flappy Bird game in Python. You must include these things:\n1. You must use pygame.\n2. The background color should be randomly chosen and is a light shade. Start with a light blue color.\n3. Pressing SPACE multiple times will accelerate the bird.\n4. The bird's shape should be randomly chosen as a square, circle or triangle. The color should be randomly chosen as a dark color.\n5. Place on the bottom some land colored as dark brown or yellow chosen randomly.\n6. Make a score shown on the top right side. Increment if you pass pipes and don't hit them.\n7. Make randomly spaced pipes with enough space. Color them randomly as dark green or light brown or a dark gray shade.\n8. When you lose, show the best score. Make the text inside the screen. Pressing q or Esc will quit the game. Restarting is pressing SPACE again.\nThe final game should be inside a markdown section in Python. Check your code for errors and fix them before the final markdown section.<\|im_end\|><\|im_assistant\|>assistant<\|im_middle\|>"<br/>```<br/><br/>## <br/><br/>🔍Tokenizer quirks and bug fixes<br/><br/>The Kimi K2 tokenizer was interesting to play around with - **it's mostly similar in action to GPT-4o's tokenizer**! We first see in the tokenization\_kimi.py file the following regular expression (regex) that Kimi K2 uses:<br/><br/>Copy<br/><br/>```<br/>pat_str = "\|".join(<br/>    [<br/>        r"""[\p\{Han\}]+""",<br/>        r"""[^\r\n\p\{L\}\p\{N\}]?[\p\{Lu\}\p\{Lt\}\p\{Lm\}\p\{Lo\}\p\{M\}&&[^\p\{Han\}]]*[\p\{Ll\}\p\{Lm\}\p\{Lo\}\p\{M\}&&[^\p\{Han\}]]+(?i:'s\|'t\|'re\|'ve\|'m\|'ll\|'d)?""",<br/>        r"""[^\r\n\p\{L\}\p\{N\}]?[\p\{Lu\}\p\{Lt\}\p\{Lm\}\p\{Lo\}\p\{M\}&&[^\p\{Han\}]]+[\p\{Ll\}\p\{Lm\}\p\{Lo\}\p\{M\}&&[^\p\{Han\}]]*(?i:'s\|'t\|'re\|'ve\|'m\|'ll\|'d)?""",<br/>        r"""\p\{N\}{1,3\}""",<br/>        r""" ?[^\s\p\{L\}\p\{N\}]+[\r\n]*""",<br/>        r"""\s*[\r\n]+""",<br/>        r"""\s+(?!\S)""",<br/>        r"""\s+""",<br/>    ]<br/>)<br/>```<br/><br/>After careful inspection, we find Kimi K2 is nearly identical to GPT-4o's tokenizer regex which can be found in llama.cpp's source code.<br/><br/>Copy<br/><br/>```<br/>[^\r\n\p\{L\}\p\{N\}]?[\p\{Lu\}\p\{Lt\}\p\{Lm\}\p\{Lo\}\p\{M\}]*[\p\{Ll\}\p\{Lm\}\p\{Lo\}\p\{M\}]+(?i:'s\|'t\|'re\|'ve\|'m\|'ll\|'d)?\|[^\r\n\p\{L\}\p\{N\}]?[\p\{Lu\}\p\{Lt\}\p\{Lm\}\p\{Lo\}\p\{M\}]+[\p\{Ll\}\p\{Lm\}\p\{Lo\}\p\{M\}]*(?i:'s\|'t\|'re\|'ve\|'m\|'ll\|'d)?\|\p\{N\}{1,3\}\| ?[^\s\p\{L\}\p\{N\}]+[\r\n/]*\|\s*[\r\n]+\|\s+(?!\S)\|\s+<br/>```<br/><br/>Both tokenize numbers into groups of 1 to 3 numbers (9, 99, 999), and use similar patterns. The only difference looks to be the handling of "Han" or Chinese characters, which Kimi's tokenizer deals with more. The PR by <https://github.com/gabriellarson> handles these differences well after some discussions here.<br/><br/>**We also find the correct EOS token should not be \[EOS\], but rather <\|im\_end\|>, which we have also fixed in our model conversions.**<br/><br/>## <br/><br/>🐦 Flappy Bird + other tests<br/><br/>We introduced the Flappy Bird test when our 1.58bit quants for DeepSeek R1 were provided. We found Kimi K2 one of the only models to one-shot all our tasks including this one, Heptagon and others tests even at 2-bit. The goal is to ask the LLM to create a Flappy Bird game but following some specific instructions:<br/><br/>Copy<br/><br/>```<br/>Create a Flappy Bird game in Python. You must include these things:<br/>1. You must use pygame.<br/>2. The background color should be randomly chosen and is a light shade. Start with a light blue color.<br/>3. Pressing SPACE multiple times will accelerate the bird.<br/>4. The bird's shape should be randomly chosen as a square, circle or triangle. The color should be randomly chosen as a dark color.<br/>5. Place on the bottom some land colored as dark brown or yellow chosen randomly.<br/>6. Make a score shown on the top right side. Increment if you pass pipes and don't hit them.<br/>7. Make randomly spaced pipes with enough space. Color them randomly as dark green or light brown or a dark gray shade.<br/>8. When you lose, show the best score. Make the text inside the screen. Pressing q or Esc will quit the game. Restarting is pressing SPACE again.<br/>The final game should be inside a markdown section in Python. Check your code for errors and fix them before the final markdown section.<br/>```<br/><br/>You can also test the dynamic quants via the Heptagon Test as per r/Localllama which tests the model on creating a basic physics engine to simulate balls rotating in a moving enclosed heptagon shape.<br/><br/>The goal is to make the heptagon spin, and the balls in the heptagon should move. The prompt is below:<br/><br/>Copy<br/><br/>```<br/>Write a Python program that shows 20 balls bouncing inside a spinning heptagon:\n- All balls have the same radius.\n- All balls have a number on it from 1 to 20.\n- All balls drop from the heptagon center when starting.\n- Colors are: #f8b862, #f6ad49, #f39800, #f08300, #ec6d51, #ee7948, #ed6d3d, #ec6800, #ec6800, #ee7800, #eb6238, #ea5506, #ea5506, #eb6101, #e49e61, #e45e32, #e17b34, #dd7a56, #db8449, #d66a35\n- The balls should be affected by gravity and friction, and they must bounce off the rotating walls realistically. There should also be collisions between balls.\n- The material of all the balls determines that their impact bounce height will not exceed the radius of the heptagon, but higher than ball radius.\n- All balls rotate with friction, the numbers on the ball can be used to indicate the spin of the ball.\n- The heptagon is spinning around its center, and the speed of spinning is 360 degrees per 5 seconds.\n- The heptagon size should be large enough to contain all the balls.\n- Do not use the pygame library; implement collision detection algorithms and collision response etc. by yourself. The following Python libraries are allowed: tkinter, math, numpy, dataclasses, typing, sys.\n- All codes should be put in a single Python file.<br/>```<br/><br/>PreviousGemma 3n: How to Run & Fine-tuneNextDeepSeek-R1-0528: How to Run Locally<br/><br/>Last updated 0 minutes ago<br/><br/>LogoLogo<br/><br/>#### Community<br/><br/>* Reddit r/unsloth<br/>* Twitter (X)<br/>* LinkedIn<br/><br/>#### Resources<br/><br/>* Blog<br/>* Tutorials<br/>* Hugging Face<br/><br/>#### Company<br/><br/>* About<br/>* Events<br/>* Contact |

K2 is fully transparent, meaning we’ve open-sourced all artifacts, including code, data, model checkpoints, intermediate results, and more.<br/><br/>k2 eval table<br/><br/>##  About K2: <br/><br/>* 65 billion parameter LLM<br/>* Tokens: 1.4T<br/>* Languages: English<br/>* Models Released: base, chat model<br/>* Trained in 2 stages<br/>* License: Apache 2.0<br/><br/>K2 was developed as a collaboration between MBZUAI, Petuum, and LLM360.<br/><br/>##  LLM360 Model Performance and Evaluation Collection <br/><br/>The LLM360 Performance and Evaluation Collection is a robust evaluations set consisting of general and domain specific evaluations to assess model knowledge and function. <br/><br/>Evaluations include standard best practice benchmarks, medical, math, and coding knowledge. More about the evaluations can be found here.<br/><br/>k2 big eval table<br/><br/>Detailed analysis can be found on the K2 Weights and Biases project here<br/><br/>##  Open LLM Leaderboard <br/><br/>\| Evaluation \| Score \| Raw Score \|<br/>\| ---------- \| ----- \| --------- \|<br/>\| IFEval     \| 22.52 \| 23        \|<br/>\| BBH        \| 28.22 \| 50        \|<br/>\| Math Lvl 5 \| 2.04  \| 2         \|<br/>\| GPQA       \| 3.58  \| 28        \|<br/>\| MUSR       \| 8.55  \| 40        \|<br/>\| MMLU-PRO   \| 22.27 \| 30        \|<br/>\| Average    \| 14.53 \| 35.17     \|<br/><br/>##  K2 Gallery <br/><br/>The K2 gallery allows one to browse the output of various prompts on intermediate K2 checkpoints, which provides an intuitive understanding on how the model develops and improves over time. tutorials for tech enthusiasts, AI practitioners and academic or industry researchers who want to learn pretraining techniques here.<br/><br/>##  LLM360 Developer Suite <br/><br/>We provide     Ask Gemini a question and get a response grounded with Google Search results.

*Result*:
🤖 GEMINI RESEARCH RESPONSE:

The Kimi K2 model by Moonshot AI can be run in several ways, including:

*   **Using the Kimi Platform API:** The Kimi Platform offers an OpenAI/Anthropic-compatible API.
*   **Running within Claude Code:** Kimi K2 can be run directly within the Claude Code environment.
*   **Running locally:** You can run Kimi K2 on your local device.
    *   The full Kimi K2 Q8 quant requires 1.09TB of disk space and at least 8 x H200 GPUs.
    *   The quantized Unsloth Dynamic 1.8-bit version reduces the disk space requirement to 245GB.
    *   You need at least 250GB of disk space to run the 1-bit quant.
    *   The 1.8-bit quant can fit in a 1x 24GB GPU (with all MoE layers offloaded to system RAM or a fast disk).
*   **Using inference engines:** It's recommended to run Kimi K2 on one of the following inference engines: vLLM, SGLang, KTransformers, or TensorRT-LLM.

To run Kimi K2, it is also important to consider the following points:

*   **API Key:** You need to register on the Moonshot AI Platform and generate an API key.
*   **Environment Variables:** Set up your environment variables by exporting your API key.
*   **Software:** Obtain the latest llama.cpp on GitHub.
*   **Hardware:** The only requirement is disk space + RAM + VRAM ≥ 250GB.
*   **Settings:** Set the temperature to 0.6 to reduce repetition and incoherence. It is also recommended to set min\_p to 0.01 to suppress the errors.

</details>

---

Here are the main ways to run the new K2 LLM model (including both the Kimi K2 by Moonshot AI and the LLM360/K2 model), based on the latest web and Gemini research:

---

## 1. Run Locally (Linux, Windows, Mac)

### a. Using Hugging Face Transformers (Python)
- **Requirements:** Python 3.10+, CUDA-compatible GPU (A100, 3090, 4090 or better), Linux/WSL2, lots of disk space (245GB+ for quantized, 1TB+ for full model), and RAM+VRAM ≥ quant size.
- **Steps:**
  1. Create a Python environment (conda or venv).
  2. Install dependencies:
     ```bash
     pip install torch torchvision torchaudio --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cu121
     pip install transformers accelerate huggingface_hub
     ```
  3. Clone the model repo:
     ```bash
     git lfs install
     git clone https://huggingface.co/moonshotai/Kimi-K2-Instruct
     cd Kimi-K2-Instruct
     ```
  4. Load and run the model in Python:
     ```python
     from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer
     import torch
     model_id = "moonshotai/Kimi-K2-Instruct"
     tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_id, trust_remote_code=True)
     model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(model_id, trust_remote_code=True, torch_dtype=torch.float16).cuda()
     prompt = "Explain the theory of relativity in simple terms."
     inputs = tokenizer(prompt, return_tensors="pt").to("cuda")
     outputs = model.generate(**inputs, max_new_tokens=200)
     print(tokenizer.decode(outputs[0], skip_special_tokens=True))
     ```

### b. Using vLLM, SGLang, TensorRT-LLM, or KTransformers
- These are high-performance inference engines for large models.
- Example (vLLM):
  ```bash
  pip install vllm
  python3 -m vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server \
    --model moonshotai/Kimi-K2-Instruct \
    --tokenizer moonshotai/Kimi-K2-Instruct
  ```
- For TensorRT-LLM, use Docker and GPU passthrough for multi-node setups.

### c. Using llama.cpp (for quantized GGUF models)
- Download the quantized model (e.g., Unsloth Dynamic 1.8-bit, 245GB).
- Build llama.cpp with CUDA if you have a GPU.
- Run with:
  ```bash
  ./llama.cpp/llama-cli \
    --model unsloth/Kimi-K2-Instruct-GGUF/UD-TQ1_0/Kimi-K2-Instruct-UD-TQ1_0-00001-of-00005.gguf \
    --cache-type-k q4_0 \
    --threads -1 \
    --n-gpu-layers 99 \
    --temp 0.6 \
    --min_p 0.01 \
    --ctx-size 16384 \
    --seed 3407 \
    -ot ".ffn_.*_exps.=CPU" \
    --prompt "<|im_system|>system<|im_middle|>You are a helpful assistant<|im_end|><|im_user|>user<|im_middle|>What is 1+1?<|im_end|>"
  ```
- Disk offloading is supported if you don't have enough RAM/VRAM, but it will be slower.

---

## 2. Run via API (Cloud)
- **Moonshot AI Platform:** Register and get an API key, then use their OpenAI/Anthropic-compatible API endpoint.
- **Environment:** Set your API key as an environment variable.
- **Usage:** Use the API in your code or via command-line tools.

---

## 3. Run in LM Studio or Ollama (for smaller/quantized versions)
- **LM Studio:** Download and run models with a GUI.
- **Ollama:** Command-line tool for running LLMs locally (mainly for smaller models).

---

## 4. Run in Claude Code or similar cloud environments
- Some platforms (like Claude Code) may support direct execution of K2 models.

---

## 5. LLM360/K2 Model (65B)
- Available on Hugging Face: https://huggingface.co/LLM360/K2
- Load with Transformers:
  ```python
  from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer
  tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("LLM360/K2")
  model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained("LLM360/K2")
  prompt = 'what is the highest mountain on earth?'
  input_ids = tokenizer(prompt, return_tensors="pt").input_ids
  gen_tokens = model.generate(input_ids, do_sample=True, max_new_tokens=128)
  print(tokenizer.batch_decode(gen_tokens)[0])
  ```

---

## Hardware & Performance Notes
- For full Kimi K2: 1TB+ disk, 250GB+ RAM+VRAM, 8x H200 GPUs for best speed.
- For quantized: 245GB disk, 24GB GPU (with offloading), or use disk offloading for slower performance.
- Set temperature to 0.6 and min_p to 0.01 for best results.

---

- [Unsloth Kimi K2 Local Guide](https://docs.unsloth.ai/basics/kimi-k2-how-to-run-locally)

- [LLM360/K2 on Hugging Face](https://huggingface.co/LLM360/K2)
